register: Private aviation operators — plain English, no insider jargon. NEVER use: 'Part 135', 'ARGUS', 'Wyvern', 'fractional ownership', 'jet card', 'empty-leg', 'flight department KPI', 'HNW', 'FBO'. NEVER use personal-name tokens ({first_name}, {prospect_name}) — calling businesses, not individuals. NEVER use Miner-style apology hedges ('appreciate you picking up', 'out of the blue', 'I know this is out of nowhere'). NEVER use casual/slang permission ('got 90 seconds', 'got a minute', 'grab someone', 'cool?', 'shoot me your email'). NEVER use 'Quick on me'. NEVER use {company} in beat copy — use 'your site' instead. Tone: discreet, surgical, neutral, composed. No hype, no slang, no emoji, no high-pressure. PAS + Jeremy Miner NEPQ + neuromarketing structure. Close on booked call OR email-for-sample-audit.

A.name: Dollar-Anchored Leak
A.desc: PAS + NEPQ. Opens with the dollar-loss anchor on the quote page. Walks the buyer journey, holds back the third issue for the audit. Best for operators running ads where the leak is page-load + hero + phone placement.

beats.title: Dollar-Anchored Leak
beats.line: Hi, my name is {caller} with ConversionJet — I review private charter sites for a living. I pulled up your site, and there's a problem on your quote page costing you roughly $25,000 to $75,000 a week in booked charters. Do you know who I can talk to about this?
beats.followup: Got it — 60 seconds and you'll see exactly where it's going.
beats.note: Earned attention via research. No name token. No Miner hedges. Pause after the dollar number.

beats.title: The 3-Second Mobile Window
beats.line: Quick context — when a buyer pulls up a charter site from their phone, they decide in about 3 seconds whether you're worth waiting on. Every second past that cuts bookings around 20 percent. Most charter sites load in 15 to 20 seconds on mobile. When was the last time someone on your team pulled your site up on a phone and requested a quote end-to-end?
beats.followup: Most operators haven't tested it themselves in months. That's the issue.
beats.note: Mobile-first question. The 'when was the last time' lands hard because they almost never have.

beats.title: Buyer Walks Away
beats.line: So here's what's happening on your site. That same buyer from the back of the car — they tap your ad. Page takes 12 to 15 seconds to load. By the time it renders, they're already losing patience. First thing they see? A headline that doesn't mention their route, their aircraft, or a price. By second 8, they're scrolling for a phone number — yours isn't pinned in the top right, so they hunt for it. By the time they find it buried in the footer, they've already opened the next operator in another tab. That's where the $25,000 to $75,000 a week goes. Two fixes — pinning the phone, rewriting the hero — are afternoon work for a developer. There's a third issue on your quote flow costing you more than both combined. I'd rather walk you through that one with your page open in front of us.
responses: How long does the audit take?
beats.followup: I'd rather show you on a shared screen — it's faster than describing it.
beats.note: Hold back the third issue. Curiosity gap drives the audit ask.

beats.title: 100-Site Checklist
beats.line: A bit about me, I've diagnosed over 100 charter and aircraft sites against the same 10-point checklist. Same conversion math every time — trip values get bigger, speed-to-quote window shorter. The two issues I named on your site aren't theory; they're the same two I've fixed before.
responses: Who else have you worked with?
beats.followup: I'll show you the checklist on the call. That's actually what the audit is.
beats.note: Credibility without name-dropping. Builds the bridge to the audit ask.

beats.title: $3K Audit, Free
beats.line: Here's where I'd take this. In 30 minutes, shared screen, your website open in front of us. I'll walk through every issue in plain English, name the dollars lost on each one, and you keep the prioritized fix-list. The audit runs $3,000 — but, I'll run it for free. The only thing you have to lose is the $25,000 to $75,000 a week in lost charters from the issues I named. No pitch on the call. By minute 20 most operators already know if they're running the fixes in-house or would prefer having me handle it.
beats.followup: No catch — I get to see what's actually breaking on charter sites, you get the fix-list.
beats.note: $3K anchor is the value frame. 'No pitch' kills the catch reflex.

beats.title: Dual Path — Call or Email
beats.line: Two ways we can do this. Either I book you in for the 30 minutes this week — I've got Thursday at 10 or Friday at 2, Central — or I send you a sample audit I did for another charter operator so you can see exactly what you'd get before we ever talk. Which makes more sense for you?
beats.followup: Perfect — what's the best email for the calendar invite (or sample)?
beats.note: Dual-path close. Assumptive on calendar. Email is the soft fallback.

B.name: Peer Comparison
B.desc: Status loss + FOMO. Opens with the peer benchmark — three competitors in market, all beating them on speed-to-quote. Best for operators with ego in the game (Owners, Directors of Sales).

beats.title: Peer Comparison
beats.line: Hi, my name is {caller} with ConversionJet — I review private charter sites for a living. I just finished comparing your site against three other operators in your market, and you're losing to all three on the one thing that decides who gets the booking. Do you know who I can talk to about this?
beats.followup: I'd rather walk you through it — 60 seconds and you'll see exactly where they're ahead.
beats.note: Status loss frame. Hold back the named competitors until the audit. Plural 'three' is the curiosity hook.

beats.title: Side-by-Side Load Gap
beats.line: Here's where they're losing it. Their pages load in 3 to 4 seconds, ask for a route, show a price range. Yours takes 14, asks for a phone number, drops a contact form. The buyer is already typing into a competitor's quote form before your site finishes loading.
beats.followup: I ran the load times myself on mobile this morning — happy to show you on the audit.
beats.note: Concrete side-by-side punch. No 'three competitors' restated — already said in Beat 1.

beats.title: Monthly Math
beats.line: Now do the math on a month of this. Say 20 buyers a week hit your ad and your competitors' ads at the same time. 14 of them never wait out your load. The 6 that do see a page that doesn't sell them. The other operators? Same 20 buyers, three-second load, route and price on screen. They're booking 12 of yours every month. That's the $25,000 to $75,000 a week — except it's not a week, it's a compounding lead off your top line.
beats.followup: Conservative actually — happy to walk you through the math on the audit.
beats.note: Zooms out from one buyer to monthly volume. The 'compounding' kicker raises stakes.

beats.title: Same Playbook
beats.line: A bit about me — I've diagnosed over 100 charter and aircraft sites against the same 10-point checklist. The three operators ahead of your site aren't doing anything you can't. They just closed the gap I'm describing. It's the same playbook every time.
responses: How fast can we close the gap?
beats.followup: I'll show you the full playbook on the call — it's literally what the audit is.
beats.note: Reframes the gap as closeable, not structural. Removes the 'we're too far behind' objection.

beats.title: $3K Audit, Free
beats.line: Here's where I'd take this. In 30 minutes, shared screen, your website open in front of us. I'll walk through every issue in plain English, name the dollars lost on each one, and you keep the prioritized fix-list. The audit runs $3,000 — but, I'll run it for free. The only thing you have to lose is the $25,000 to $75,000 a week in lost charters from the issues I named. No pitch on the call. By minute 20 most operators already know if they're running the fixes in-house or would prefer having me handle it.
beats.followup: No catch — I get to see what's actually breaking on charter sites, you get the fix-list.
beats.note: Identical to Variant A. Locked brand voice.

beats.title: Dual Path — Call or Email
beats.line: Two ways we can do this. Either I book you in for the 30 minutes this week — I've got Thursday at 10 or Friday at 2, Central — or I send you a sample audit I did for another charter operator so you can see exactly what you'd get before we ever talk. Which makes more sense for you?
beats.followup: Perfect — what's the best email for the calendar invite (or sample)?

C.name: Hidden Quote-Flow Issue
C.desc: Curiosity gap + loss aversion. Opens with the bigger quote-flow problem A only teased. Best for operators who think their site is already fine — defangs the 'we just redesigned' objection.

beats.title: Hidden Quote-Flow Issue
beats.line: Hi, my name is {caller} with ConversionJet — I review private charter sites for a living. I saw your company is running ads for private charter flights, I pulled up your site this morning, and noticed an issue on your quote request flow — it's costing you more than every other problem on the page combined. Do you know who I can talk to about this?
beats.followup: I'd rather walk you through it — 60 seconds and you'll see exactly where the leak is.
beats.note: Earned-attention via ads research. Hold back the specific issue until Beat 3 — curiosity gap drives the call.

beats.title: Quote Form Is the Bleeder
beats.line: Quick context — you're paying $40 to $90 per click to get a buyer to your page, and the quote request form is where 80 percent of those clicks die. Buyers will sit through a slow page, a generic headline, a buried phone number — but the moment the form asks them something it shouldn't, or asks it in the wrong order, they're gone. And the buyers booking the biggest trips are the first ones to leave.
beats.followup: Bigger trip = more cautious buyer. I'll show you the exact pattern on the audit.
beats.note: CPC anchor makes the leak feel live, not theoretical. Sets up Beat 3 reveal.

beats.title: Form Friction Walked
beats.line: Here's what's happening on your quote flow. The form opens by asking for name, phone, and email — before it asks anything about the trip. You're demanding contact info from a buyer who hasn't decided to talk to you yet. The ones who push through get to the trip section, and the form asks for departure city as free text. No autocomplete. They start typing, second-guess the format, and leave. That's the $25,000 to $75,000 a week. Every $40 to $90 click you paid for is funding it.
beats.followup: I'd rather walk through the fix with your page open — happens fast on a shared screen.
beats.note: Concrete two-issue reveal. Industry-standard objection is the most common — followup deflects to the audit.

beats.title: Same Pattern, Fixed Before
beats.line: A bit about me — I've diagnosed over 100 charter and aircraft sites against the same 10-point checklist. The quote-flow pattern I just walked you through isn't unique to your site. I've fixed it before, and the conversion math holds every time.
responses: Who else have you fixed it for?
How long does the fix take?
beats.followup: I'll show you the checklist on the audit — that's actually what the 30 minutes is.
beats.note: Pattern-recognition credibility. Same playbook frame as B.

beats.title: $3K Audit, Free
beats.line: Here's where I'd take this. In 30 minutes, shared screen, your website open in front of us. I'll walk through every issue in plain English, name the dollars lost on each one, and you keep the prioritized fix-list. The audit runs $3,000 — but, I'll run it for free. The only thing you have to lose is the $25,000 to $75,000 a week in lost charters from the issues I named. No pitch on the call. By minute 20 most operators already know if they're running the fixes in-house or would prefer having me handle it.
beats.followup: No catch — I get to see what's actually breaking on charter sites, you get the fix-list.

beats.title: Dual Path — Call or Email
beats.line: Two ways we can do this. Either I book you in for the 30 minutes this week — I've got Thursday at 10 or Friday at 2, Central — or I send you a sample audit I did for another charter operator so you can see exactly what you'd get before we ever talk. Which makes more sense for you?
beats.followup: Perfect — what's the best email for the calendar invite (or sample)?
